words: SAM SCHRAGER: A little bit of background on before you came to Bovill. The
first thing I noticed on that background sheet is that you grew up in
South Dakota and Saskatchewan. Were your parents homesteading?
JOSEPH HOLLAND: Yes, they were farmers in South Dakota, and then they homesteaded
in Saskatchewan. Yeah, I was a farm boy until I come to Spokane.
That was the end of farming.

speaker: SAM
words: Did your whole family leave Saskatchewan?

speaker: JH
words: Yes, yes. I left just a few months before them, and went back to South

speaker: JH
words: Dakota for, well, from about July to December. I worked there and then

speaker: JH
words: I joined them.in Spokane. They came to Spokane, I'd say about in Oct

speaker: JH
words: ober of that year. Yeah, they sold out. And then, I said on the back-

speaker: JH
words: ground sheet, went up there at the telegraph school- Spokane Telegraph

speaker: JH
words: Institute it was called— finished that, and then started working for

speaker: JH
words: the railroad, Great Northern. I worked right in Spokane for a while,

speaker: JH
words: right down there on Havermale Island, where the fair now is, in the build

speaker: JH
words: ing where the tower is left. Oh, I worked quite a few places on the

speaker: JH
words: Great Northern— Priest River, Newport and west over to Harrington— Oh,

speaker: JH
words: I never got as far as Wenatehee— Edwall, and Lyons. Well, then the

speaker: JH
words: next spring there wasn't much work for extra board, and a new man, you

speaker: JH
words: see, you're on the extra board. So this Inland Empire, which runs be

speaker: JH
words: tween Spokane, Moscow and Colfax, they needed somebody so I went to work

speaker: JH
words: for them. I said in there, Iwas cashier and operator different places,

speaker: JH
words: and relief agent, and wound up in Moscow. I was cashier and operator

speaker: JH
words: there. Stayed for about two years. And then this job at Bovill came

speaker: JH
words: up as joint agent, and it was abetter paying job, so Ijust quit and

speaker: JH
words: took it. The railroad at Moscow was very good about it, too. When I

speaker: JH
words: was leaving they said, "Well, we moved you in here" they do that, you

speaker: JH
words: know, your furniture and anything you have- "moved you in here and

speaker: JH
words: we'll move you out." So I had free transportation of my household

speaker: JH
words: goods, myself and my family.

speaker: JH
words: Well, that's when Istarted in at Bovill. Oh, things were humming

speaker: JH
words: there then. The roads into Bovill, in the wintertime, there just wasn't

speaker: JH
words: anything. The snow covered and they never tried to keep 'em open. Peo

speaker: JH
words: ple would put their cars up on blocks, oh, sometimes as early as October,

speaker: JH
words: November, when winter hadn't set in hardly, and they were there until

speaker: JH
words: the next May or more. Iheard some say that that was apretty good thing,

speaker: JH
words: they got achance to catch up on their grocery bill, they wasn't spend

speaker: JH
words: ing so much for gasoline. Left thedepot every time the trains came in,

speaker: JH
words: which was shortly after noon, one-something, the Milwaukee come up there,

speaker: JH
words: and the WISH passenger train met. And oh, mail and everything that

speaker: JH
words: came into the town came on the train. So quite often there was alot of

speaker: JH
words: people gathered around the depot, that just seemed to be adaily event.

speaker: JH
words: Gather around there to see the trains come in and go. Two or three dray

speaker: JH
words: teams backed up against the platform to take the mail, express, and what

speaker: JH
words: not, deliver it over the town.

speaker: JH
words: The winters there sometimes the snow used to get, oh, real deep. They

speaker: JH
words: still have snow there, but it's nothing like it was in those days. It

speaker: JH
words: seems to me, that about, oh, by the fifteenth of December that fence that

speaker: JH
words: was out in front of the depot- it was agood five and ahalf feet highbut

speaker: JH
words: that would be covered. The posts would disappear, and you wouldn't

speaker: JH
words: see them again until March, maybe, when the snow started down. And

speaker: JH
words: the railroads, it was.quite a job there plowing out the tracks, the

speaker: JH
words: side tracks^ and we had all kinds of equipment to do it. Sometimes

speaker: JH
words: three or four engines stalled, snowed in, take one to pull another out.

speaker: JH
words: They'd get going, oh, they'd get through it. And it was the same every

speaker: JH
words: winter there for quite a while, and then finally the winters wasn't

speaker: JH
words: quite so severe, less snow.

speaker: JH
words: 'Course, sometimes the logging company would be down for a few

speaker: JH
words: months in the winter, in those days. But now it's kind of reversed: they

speaker: JH
words: log now in the winter, but spring come, then they have their layoff for

speaker: JH
words: maybe a couple of months. Things are wet in the woods. Those days it

speaker: JH
words: was all horse logging. Potlatch Lumber Company, which it was called

speaker: JH
words: at the time, they had oh two or three huge barns across the meadow

speaker: JH
words: from the depot where they would keep their horses when they were not

speaker: JH
words: working. And they never had a horse that wouldn't weigh at least a ton,

speaker: JH
words: and a lot of them weighing twenty-one, twenty-two hundred pounds. Big

speaker: JH
words: horses. But I guess it takes big horses to do heavy work in the woods.

speaker: JH
words: Yeah, then all at once there was no more horses, they started using

speaker: JH
words: cats. They sold those horses as they could. Some of the farmers from

speaker: JH
words: around the country would come in and buy 'em. I think they said they

speaker: JH
words: had two hundred sixty head there at one time, just wintering. That's

speaker: JH
words: a lot of horses. Took a fair sized crew of men to just take care of

speaker: JH
words: 'em. But then they'd do anything to get rid of them. They'd sell 'em

speaker: JH
words: for whatever people wanted to give, and then some even they shipped,

speaker: JH
words: loaded them in stock cars and shipped 'em to Portland, I think it was,

speaker: JH
words: where they would be slaughtered, and I don't know what they would be

speaker: JH
words: used for. They used to say they were going to the glue factory.

speaker: SAM
words: Did many of the lumberjacks winter over?

speaker: JH
words: A lot of them would come to Spokane. This was kind of their headquar

speaker: JH
words: ters, see. A lot of the single lumberjacks would come to Spokane. Of

speaker: JH
words: course the family people and then some of the others would remain there

speaker: JH
words: in Bovill in a shack or two, you know. Out on the edges of town they

speaker: JH
words: would be shacking up, different ones, just waiting for spring. But

speaker: JH
words: the big majority of those single men, they'd hike out to Spokane. But

speaker: JH
words: they always kept in touch, and next spring when it opened, they'd be

speaker: JH
words: back.

speaker: SAM
words: So they really had a lot of continuity from one year to the other, a

speaker: SAM
words: lot of the same people would be coming back in.

speaker: JH
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, they did. Well, the foAan and the superintendent

speaker: JH
words: and all of them, you know, they get to know certain lumberjacks, and

speaker: JH
words: they know they can do the work. They like 'em, and they like to have

speaker: JH
words: em. Old lumberjack is a lumberjack and they just don't make 'em

speaker: JH
words: anymore! No, there's no lumberjacks, because they were the single

speaker: JH
words: men, and for quite a number of years now, the woods work is done by

speaker: JH
words: young married people who drive their cars to work and whatnot. Drive

speaker: JH
words: as close as they can to the work. But oh, yeah, those old fellas— a

speaker: JH
words: lot of them were sawyers, that's when they used crosscut saws, one

speaker: JH
words: on each end of it. That's a thing of the past too. Trees are all

speaker: JH
words: felled now by power saws. So that element in the woods isn't there..

speaker: JH
words: any more. One young fella with a power saw'11 knock down more trees

speaker: JH
words: in a half a day than, why, I don't know how many good men with hand

speaker: JH
words: saws could do in three or four days. There's just that much differ

speaker: JH
words: ence.

speaker: SAM
words: Was being tough, being able to get out there and take it and put in a

speaker: SAM
words: real long day's work, was that really important to the lumberjacks

speaker: SAM
words: themselves?

speaker: JH
words: OH, I don't think so, you know. No. The weather was never that bad.

speaker: JH
words: You worked long hours, but when you go back—well, we're going back

speaker: JH
words: about forty-nine years now, seeing I came to Bovill in 1925— every

speaker: JH
words: body worked long hours. Even when I left the depot at Moscow, I

speaker: JH
words: was working ten hours a day. When I went to Bovill, however, it was

speaker: JH
words: eight hours, and the eight hour day in the woods was coming in. Oh,

speaker: JH
words: they worked long hours. A lot of them, you see, sawyers, and more

speaker: JH
words: than sawyers, it was so much a foot, so much a thousand. So

speaker: JH
words: they didn't mind the long hours, 'cause the more they worked, the more

speaker: JH
words: they made. There would always be a considerable percentage of the

speaker: JH
words: group that was on hourly pay, pretty small pay too. then, about oh

speaker: JH
words: thirty-two and a half cents an hour was probably a pretty good pay

speaker: JH
words: then. And a lot of the extra gangs on the railroad was only twenty five

speaker: JH
words: cents an hour. No, it didn't take any super person physically.

speaker: JH
words: Of course, doing that kind of work, you know, you get used to it and

speaker: JH
words: put out a lot of work, eat heavy.

speaker: JH
words: Those old lumberjacks, for the most of them, they could stay in

speaker: JH
words: the woods just about so long and then they had to get out of there.

speaker: JH
words: They'd get what they call that, some kind of afever, (Chuckles) not

speaker: JH
words: that they were sick, but they had to get out and go tq town, in some

speaker: JH
words: place where they could call "Timber". Of course, in those days when I

speaker: JH
words: went there it was Prohibition, so there wasn't any place around there

speaker: JH
words: that they could do that. I guess maybe that's what attracted some of

speaker: JH
words: them to Spokane in the winter. (Chuckles) Oh, yeah, the lumberjack

speaker: JH
words: would have a pretty good stake and go to town and go broke. And if

speaker: JH
words: he was known he could borrow quite a little bit. And then after he

speaker: JH
words: spent that, well then he was ready to go back to work. And the same

speaker: JH
words: thing over and over and over. They were regarded as pretty honorable

speaker: JH
words: men. There didn't seem to be any bad ones at all. They were good,

speaker: JH
words: help everybody they could. Well, Inever worked aday in the woods.

speaker: JH
words: I heard lots of things about 'em, you know. Some of them would darn

speaker: JH
words: their socks when they started to wear out at the heel, they'd roll

speaker: JH
words: it a little ways, and when that wore, roll it again, roll it, and keep

speaker: JH
words: rolling it until finally it would cut right in two. Well, then they'd

speaker: JH
words: have to get a new pair of socks. Now they would do that, but then

speaker: JH
words: still when they'd.go to town, money was nothing. Free, timber, (Chu

speaker: JH
words: ckles)want to treat everybody. And there were really no fights started

speaker: JH
words: amongst the lumberjacks, they didn't do that.

speaker: JH
words: Those extra gangs they used to have on the railroad though, that

speaker: JH
words: was a little different. About every year there'd be an extra gang

speaker: JH
words: come up, and they'd have their own cars on the railroad, bunk cars,

speaker: JH
words: probably about a hundred men in the camp. And they would renew ties,

speaker: JH
words: that was their main work. But they picked those up from everywhere,

speaker: JH
words: you know, get 'em fresh out of jail or whatnot, to just go out there

speaker: JH
words: for twenty-five cents an hour. But they did get their board, bed and

speaker: JH
words: a place to sleep. Oh, they were coming and going all the time. But

speaker: JH
words: some of these were pretty rough characters. (Chuckles) There was one

speaker: JH
words: of them, I looked out the window of the depot one time, and I seen

speaker: JH
words: him laying with his head across the rail up near the water tank, and

speaker: JH
words: there was a train coming pretty soon. So I hiked up there and it wasn t

speaker: JH
words: too far. He was dead drunk, and I pulled him off the rails, pulled him

speaker: JH
words: off by the feet, and he woke up a little bit, and I told him, I says,

speaker: JH
words: "Fella you got your neck right over that rail", I says, "there's a

speaker: JH
words: train coming along here right pretty quick that'll just cut your head

speaker: JH
words: off!" "Oh," he said, "that'd be all right." And I think ke meant it!

speaker: JH
words: (Chuckles) Well, I dragged him around back of the water tank, and then

speaker: JH
words: it seemed like he kinda went back to sleep and I went to the depot.

speaker: JH
words: Kept my eyes peeled, and it was only five minutes or so till the train

speaker: JH
words: came. And they couldn't have stopped.

speaker: JH
words: Yeah, that was a different class. They were a different class of

speaker: JH
words: people altogether than the ones in the woods. They were honorable men,

speaker: JH
words: the lumberjacks. But when the old crop retired, died off, there just

speaker: JH
words: isn't any more of them. And old Pat Malone, he probably knew every

speaker: JH
words: one of them. Superintendent there Nogle, C. G. Nogle, he knew a lot

speaker: JH
words: of them personally, and liked 'em. He knew they'd break loose once in

speaker: JH
words: a while and go on an awful drunk, but anyway you'd get 'em back in the

speaker: JH
words: woods, they were good men. So he went along with them.

speaker: JH
words: There was one oldtimer there, Ted Collins. He was a lumberjack.

speaker: JH
words: He decided to leave Bovill, and they'd had him in jail there for a

speaker: JH
words: while, more or less to sober him up. Well I was justice of the peace,

speaker: JH
words: and I used to hold court right in the depot, and that time I did. And

speaker: JH
words: it was Ted, just for being drunk. And he was looking out the window

speaker: JH
words: and there was a train going by with log flats. And he said, "If I was

speaker: JH
words: free," he said, "I'd go right out there and jump on one of those log

speaker: JH
words: flats and get out of here." I said, "Is that what you want to do, Ted?"

speaker: JH
words: "Yes." I said, "Go right ahead." He said, "You mean I'm free to do

speaker: JH
words: that?" I said, "Go right ahead, if that's what you want to do." And

speaker: JH
words: he did. You couldn't do a thing, he didn't have a dime, you know, no

speaker: JH
words: need to fine him, no need to put him back in jail. All he done was

speaker: JH
words: being dfcunk, he didn't harm anybody here. And he was gone for quite-a

speaker: JH
words: while, I don't know where he went to. But oh, it must have been a year

speaker: JH
words: afterwards, in Moscow, they found a man dead in a box car. We had a

speaker: JH
words: few people that had lived in Bovill, that then was in Moscow, and one

speaker: JH
words: of them identified that man as Ted Collins. They said, "That's Ted."

speaker: JH
words: Well, then when that was the case, they asked some of us at Bovill if

speaker: JH
words: we would come down and see what we thought. '- Well. I was surprised,

speaker: JH
words: there were quite a few knew him much better and longer than I did,

speaker: JH
words: they said, "yeah, that's Ted Collins.". But Imyself couldn't see it.

speaker: JH
words: Well first of all, when Iwent over there, Howard Short was gone, but

speaker: JH
words: his wife was there. It was at night, but she said, "We got the body

speaker: JH
words: down in the basement, and we can go down and see him. And Ilooked

speaker: JH
words: at him, and I'd look from one angle and it was just adead ringer for

speaker: JH
words: him, and you go around the other side and it wasn't. And Iasked her

speaker: JH
words: if she had atape measure there, she said, "Yeah." So we measured

speaker: JH
words: him, and he wasn't near as tall as Ihad figured this Ted Collins to

speaker: JH
words: be. So they had, you say- alittle something there, you know, if you

speaker: JH
words: thought it was Ted Collins, or if you knew who it was. Well, Iwrote

speaker: JH
words: down, "I don't know who it is, but I'm satisfied it's not Ted Collins."

speaker: JH
words: Well, another year or so went by, and by gosh here old Ted Collins

speaker: JH
words: rolls off of aflat car, walked in the depot. (Chuckles) Iwasn't

speaker: JH
words: too surprised because Iwas satisfied in my own mind that that wasn't

speaker: JH
words: Ted Collins that was buried in Moscow. And I told him, "Well," I says,

speaker: JH
words: Ted, you've been dead now for, oh, let's see(I don't know whether it

speaker: JH
words: was one or two years), one or two years." "Oh," he said, "where did

speaker: JH
words: they bury me?" "Moscow1.' "Well, well, well," he said, "I ought to be

speaker: JH
words: good for a meal then." I said, "You bet you will be!" And, I said,

speaker: JH
words: "You just go over to Mrs. Watts," she was running the restaurant, and

speaker: JH
words: I said, "you'll get a meal." And she was so glad to see him that she

speaker: JH
words: just kept him there, working around, bull cooking. He stayed there for

speaker: JH
words: a long, long time.

speaker: SAM
words: Sounds like dying was one of the best things that ever happened to him.

speaker: JH
words: Yeah, yeah, but, you know, one more detail about examining him, his

speaker: JH
words: clothes— he had different clothes, you know, than a lumberjack would

speaker: JH
words: have, like light shoes, and little rolled black, cotton socks. Well,

speaker: JH
words: lumberjacks, you know, they got generally wool socks and the wool pants

speaker: JH
words: ans whatnot. So his clothing didn't check out to me either. And then

speaker: JH
words: when Imeasured him for height, and Iasked about that, too: "Now that

speaker: JH
words: this man is dead, how tall would he be if he was living?" They said,

speaker: JH
words: "Taller, laying on that slab. But the way we're measuring him, he'd

speaker: JH
words: be taller than if he was walking around." So that was the last con

speaker: JH
words: vincing point to me. Oh, yeah. Well there was alot of those old

speaker: JH
words: fellas that way.

speaker: SAM
words: Like Ted?

speaker: JH
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Ted was probably one of the most likable ones.though.

speaker: JH
words: He was happy-go-lucky. He didn't bother anybody. But he never wanted

speaker: JH
words: to sober up. He'd get drunk, and he wanted to stay that way. But

speaker: JH
words: then they always said when he got in the woods he was as good aman

speaker: JH
words: as they had, and drinking didn't bother hto at all, until he'd come to

speaker: JH
words: town again. Ithink he was ateamster, I'm quite sure he was. They

speaker: JH
words: used to have lots of teamsters, you know. Oh, yeah, there's lots of

speaker: JH
words: other characters, you know, but you haven't thought of them for awhile,

speaker: JH
words: then it skips your mind. But the woods was full of them. Of course,

speaker: JH
words: that Depression come along then. The outfit shut down, oh Idon't

speaker: JH
words: know exactly, but Isuppose '31, and it didn't turn awheel for prob

speaker: JH
words: ably ayear and ahalf or more. It was probably about '31 when they

speaker: JH
words: pulled the pin. Oh, alot of people moved away then. Things was pret

speaker: JH
words: ty hard. There was no unemployment compensation, you know, no social

speaker: JH
words: security, that's all ahead of that. They didn't have adime, and peo

speaker: JH
words: ple, generally speaking, didn't save ahead. They would always, most

speaker: JH
words: of them, be good at the grocery store for sixty, ninety days or more

speaker: JH
words: credit. But then that was running out, and this was different, you

speaker: JH
words: know, when the company pulled the pin, it was early in the fall, early.

speaker: JH
words: It wasn't for winter coming on, so it was a different deal.

speaker: JH
words: That was pretty tough going there! however, people seemed to en-

speaker: JH
words: joy living, though. Nobody had a dime. I was fortunate in having

speaker: JH
words: the job that I did have. Before that I had a cashier and a ware

speaker: JH
words: house man, but the business was down so bad that they laid both of

speaker: JH
words: those of, and I was running the job alone. But, as I say, I was very

speaker: JH
words: fortunate to have been on the payroll all the time. And there were

speaker: JH
words: a few that were— the school teachers, and a few people that were tak

speaker: JH
words: ing care of the horses, and you about had it. Oh, yeah, there was a

speaker: JH
words: few on the railroad— the section foreman, a couple of men. But it

speaker: JH
words: was looking pretty black then. I know how it was, because I guess it

speaker: JH
words: was because I had a job that they made me relief director. Only had

speaker: JH
words: a limited amount of money, very little, to keep the town going. And

speaker: JH
words: you'd just about have to go into everybody's house to talk to them and

speaker: JH
words: see what you could do; to see, you know, maybe they had some canned

speaker: JH
words: fruit or something that another family didn't have. Well, you wouldn't

speaker: JH
words: ask them to share, but then you knew better how to distribute the

speaker: JH
words: money, trying to keep everybody alive. So it was about the most re

speaker: JH
words: warding work I believe I ever had, for self-satisfaction, I really

speaker: JH
words: think it was. Yeah.

speaker: SAM
words: How did it work out? How did you manage to distribute, things?

speaker: JH
words: Well, we formed a committee, of course, and listed everybody in town.

speaker: JH
words: And then we'd have so much money, and there was a little work, you see,

speaker: JH
words: WPA. But they were, oh I don't know, slow in getting that going; but

speaker: JH
words: when that got going then, well, like Bovill would be allocated so

speaker: JH
words: much money a month for this work. And then they'd have to say people

speaker: JH
words: with the larger family— like some of them would have as many as six

speaker: JH
words: kids— well a man like that, he'd have to work more days. Maybe he'd

speaker: JH
words: work four days a week out of the six. And then somebody that maybe

speaker: JH
words: it was just a man and wife, wel] two days at the most. There wasn't

speaker: JH
words: too many single men, they got out of there. The ones that didn't kind

speaker: JH
words: had to work for themselves. But they mostly got out of there, I don't

speaker: JH
words: know where they went to, up here,AI guess. But that's the way you had

speaker: JH
words: to kind of divide it, you know, to try to keep everybody alive.

speaker: JH
words: We coui(in't set it up "anything, it'd have to be approved. But what

speaker: JH
words: we done was road work, grading and whatnot. Our work there was mostly

speaker: JH
words: between Bovill and Elk River. 'Course, it didn't go clear to Elk River;

speaker: JH
words: went out there about seven miles. Oh, it was kind of bad. We didn't

speaker: JH
words: have anything to work with. But Potlatch Lumber Company was very good

speaker: JH
words: on loaning us their equipment without charge. Then they would have

speaker: JH
words: scrapers, you know, and they'd have picks and axes and crosscut saws

speaker: JH
words: and peaveys and shovels. It was more or less hand work. Anybody that

speaker: JH
words: had a team, we had them working hauling rock in a little old wagon of

speaker: JH
words: some kind, and some on the scrapersj but well, we got something done

speaker: JH
words: anyhow. But the jobs were manufactured, you know, just because we had

speaker: JH
words: to get some money to people and that was one way of them working for it.

speaker: JH
words: In our case the road work that we done worked out pretty good because,

speaker: JH
words: well, we had some real good men, see. Some of them that worked in the

speaker: JH
words: woods, they could do about most anything. They'd build a bridge just

speaker: JH
words: out you know, the fallen timber, trim it— they knew just how to go

speaker: JH
words: at it.

speaker: JH

speaker: SAM
words: pretty much alone unless they sort of got in his way?

speaker: JH
words: No, it was the other way around. It seemed to me that Old Pat liked to

speaker: JH
words: go after em. That was one thing that it seemed that he liked to do.

speaker: JH
words: Well, he thought he was a pretty good detective himself, you know, but

speaker: JH
words: (Chuckles) really he'd have to have a fresh snow. I think that's where

speaker: JH
words: Pat did pretty good. And he was pretty good at scaring some of them, too;

speaker: JH
words: whether he scared them or not would be another thing, but he'd always

speaker: JH
words: think he scared them out. (Chuckles) Well I don't know, oh I suppose

speaker: JH
words: he got a moonshiner or two in his life, but mostly he was for bootleg

speaker: JH
words: ging, it'd be bottle men you'd see around, selling. To catch a moon

speaker: JH
words: shiner you'd have to get out in the woods some place, you know, where

speaker: JH
words: they had a cabin or something like that. See a lot of smoke, heavy smoke,

speaker: JH
words: coming out of a chimney for a long period of time, you'd know that some

speaker: JH
words: body in there was brewing something, cooking that mash. Yeah. No, it

speaker: JH
words: always seemed to me and others too there, that that was one of Pat's.de

speaker: JH
words: lights. If he could really catch a good moonshiner or bootlegger, that

speaker: JH
words: was something he liked to do.

speaker: SAM
words: I suppose that everybody knew him so well they'd just stay out of his

speaker: SAM
words: way, if that was their business.

speaker: JH
words: Oh yes, and oh, some of them said they could follow right behind him

speaker: JH
words: if they'd see where Pat was going, then they-could walk behind, (Laughs)

speaker: JH
words: that he never looked back. I don't know, oh you'd hear things like that,

speaker: JH
words: you know, make fun of him.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, did you see much of it in town? You say that when the lumberjacks

words: came into town it was often to get drunk, these guys coming out of camp.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you see much of it?

speaker: SAM
words: I, No, you see Ididn't because, well, there was no places of business in

speaker: SAM
words: town that you could do it. Oh the poolhalls, there was acouple of

speaker: SAM
words: them, soft drinks and pool tables, card games, but oh they wouldn't

speaker: SAM
words: allow it at all, you know. It was quite apenalty, you know, you could

speaker: SAM
words: shut 'em up and put '«. out of business. No, it was never allowed in

speaker: SAM
words: those places, you didn't see it in there. Ithink really the talk about

speaker: SAM
words: the lumberjacks and going to town and getting drunk was before Prohib

speaker: SAM
words: ition. Sure you'd see it at one time, there^W of saloons in Bovill.

speaker: SAM
words: Inever seen it, it was out when Icame there. In those days that's

speaker: SAM
words: what would happen, and every other little town would be the same way.

speaker: SAM
words: Palouse was quite aplace they said, Iworked at Palouse for awhile

speaker: SAM
words: for the railroad. That along Main Street right from the Northern Pacific

speaker: SAM
words: Depot, up and back, and Idon't know how many saloons, something like,

speaker: SAM
words: oh gosh, twenty or more, twenty-seven I believe they said at one time.

speaker: SAM
words: If you made the rounds, the depot up to the end of the street and back

speaker: SAM
words: down. But Palouse was akind of jumping-off place too at one time for

speaker: SAM
words: the railroad. And that's where you get the railroad to go up the WIM

speaker: SAM
words: to the logging country, you see, Bovill. But no, Inever seen the ^

speaker: SAM
words: lumberjacks doing that, I've only heard of it. And I'm sure it's true.

speaker: SAM
words: Oh and you'd see one just like Imentioned about Ted Collins- sure you'd

speaker: SAM
words: see ted, he'd be drunk around sometimes because of alittle moonshine

speaker: SAM
words: that he'd ^et from somebody. Have it ditched somewhere, you know, out,

speaker: SAM
words: but not in the places, no. You wouldn't see them drinking it.

speaker: SAM
words: Was there much social life in Boville in the '20's when you first came?

speaker: JH
words: Oh yes, oh yes, they had amoving picture show there, they had shows oh

speaker: JH
words: three nights aweek, Isuppose. And then the upstairs part was adance

speaker: JH
words: floor, dances, two or three every month. Card parties. Oh yes, the

speaker: JH
words: social life there- and even during that Depression the social life was

speaker: JH
words: probably better than at any other time. They'd have dances there- as

speaker: JH
words: Isay, nobody had adime, you know, so the music would be volunteers. I (

speaker: JH
words: know we had one superintendent of schools that played the violin, some

speaker: JH
words: one else played something else, my wife played the piano, and it was all

speaker: JH
words: for free. And then for lunch oh people would bring little things they

speaker: JH
words: could sandwich, you know. You see there was quite alot of wild game

speaker: JH
words: around that area, deer and elk. There was alot of meat come in there

speaker: JH
words: that way. You could make up sandwiches you know with that pretty good,

speaker: JH
words: (Chuckles) you could grind it up, well little things like that. And

speaker: JH
words: then they would pass the hat, and everybody knew now, "If you don't have

speaker: JH
words: anickel, for Godsake, don't use your last nickel to put in." It was

speaker: JH
words: just to buy coffee, that was the only thing, that was the only expense,

speaker: JH
words: was to buy the coffee. Oh they had good times, good times. And then

speaker: JH
words: some you see was making home brew, and you would go to ahouse and have

speaker: JH
words: abottle of beer. Yeah, I'd say the social life during that Depression...

speaker: JH
words: And then the people stayed together better, you know. Oh before that

speaker: JH
words: some of them you know that had cars would go to Moscow, to shows or some

speaker: JH
words: thing like that; that depression come along, you didn't have that kind

speaker: JH
words: of money, you know, to drive cars. So they stayed at home and made their

speaker: JH
words: own entertainment. It was good. I've heard alot of people talk about

speaker: JH
words: that afterwards, that really, socially, that was the best times of their

speaker: JH
words: life right there, during that depression. Well you know they always

speaker: JH
words: say, "Misery likes company." Well maybe that isn't agood definition,

speaker: JH
words: but just about everybody was broke is what I'm trying to get at. So

speaker: JH
words: the guy next to you was no different. Idon't know, you could count

speaker: JH
words: the people on the payroll there on your two hands, Iknow, because the

speaker: JH
words: school teachers and myself and afew people working, taking cere of the

speaker: JH
words: horses, that was about it. Even the superintendent Sogle, they put him

speaker: JH
words: down on such alow wage, he told me, "I wouldn't miss it if Inever got

speaker: JH
words: it at all." But it was down and you could see the company didn't know

speaker: JH
words: if they'd ever get going again.

speaker: SAM
words: What was Nogle doing then?

speaker: JH
words: He was superintendent.

speaker: SAM
words: Same thing.

speaker: JH
words: Yeah, well he had nothing much to do at all then, you see, only, oh they

speaker: JH
words: had buildings there, abig warehouse and an office building. Nobody in

speaker: JH
words: the office, but abig warehouse where alot of the stuff was stored. Oh

speaker: JH
words: he didn't have much to do, but this group of men that were taking care

speaker: JH
words: of the horses, he had to supervise them, you know. Buy alittle hay and

speaker: JH
words: grain once in awhile. Ithink they said there was 260 head of horses

speaker: JH
words: there. Well they had quite afew camps around when they were working,

speaker: JH
words: and every camp was about ahundred-twenty men. Inever did know how

speaker: JH
words: many horses was in each camp though, isn't that funny? I knew the amount

speaker: JH
words: of men.

speaker: SAM
words: I was curious about the single lumberjacks that stuck around, that were

speaker: SAM
words: there logging for anumber of years. Did they sort of get adopted by

speaker: SAM
words: families? Did they have real close ties where they became part of the

speaker: SAM
words: social life of Bovill too?

speaker: JH
words: Oh in some cases yes, the single lumberjack, he had special family

speaker: JH
words: friends. Yes, that happened. But when they'd talk about going to town

speaker: JH
words: it was Spokane, you know, they wouldn't stop short of Spokane. Always

speaker: JH
words: by train too in those days. They had two ways of going there by train.

speaker: JH
words: You could go down on the WIM to Palouse, and have two chances there,

speaker: JH
words: either the Great Northern or the Northern Pacific get to Spokane; or

speaker: JH
words: they can take the Milwaukee to St. Maries and in. So they went both ways.

speaker: JH
words: Iknow one time there, this must have been about 1927 I'd say.

speaker: JH
words: We had abig fella, Charlie Albright, he died right on the basketball

speaker: JH
words: floor. We was having atown team basketball game there and Iwas man

speaker: JH
words: ager of the team. And it was anice night in the winter, February I

speaker: JH
words: think probably. And we'd had agood team that year, and this was the

speaker: JH
words: playoff, the winner would be sort of declared the champion. Ithink

speaker: JH
words: we were playing Kendrick, and the crowd,- the gym was full, anice night.

speaker: JH
words: And this Charlie, put up his hand, "Time out!", and down he went.

speaker: JH
words: We got over there, abunch of us, and he wasn't coming too, and every

speaker: JH
words: body done everything they could, you know, artificial respiration. I

speaker: JH
words: remember we got abarrel in from outside the door of the gym, and put

speaker: JH
words: him on that, rolled him back. Everybody had an idea, you know, what to

speaker: JH
words: do. And there was acouple of nurses in the room. Well one nurse said,

speaker: JH
words: "I can't feel any pulse." But the other one, whether she could or not,

speaker: JH
words: she didn't say.

speaker: JH
words: We had adoctor stationed in Bovill then, Dr. Brown, so somebody

speaker: JH
words: tore out to get Dr. Brown. That place was getting panicky, you know.

speaker: JH
words: The man was there and hadn't come to and nothing was being done. And

speaker: JH
words: then finally Dr. Brown showed up. Of course he was just afoot, oh the

speaker: JH
words: snow was awfully deep then. And he got inside the door, "Well, settle

speaker: JH
words: down," he says, "the doctor is here, the doctor's here now." And he

speaker: JH
words: walked over to him and examined him alittle bit. "Well," he said,

speaker: JH
words: "we'll have to take him up to the-", well it used to be ahospital,

speaker: JH
words: but it wasn't operating then as ahospital, but that's where the doctor's

speaker: JH
words: office was. So he said we'll have to take him up to the hospital It

speaker: JH
words: was the hospital building, see, so then they sent somebody for astret

speaker: JH
words: cher somewhere close, Iguess. Got there with the stretcher and we put

speaker: JH
words: Charlie, on it, and he was abig fella, about 240 pounds. And that snow

speaker: JH
words: the other one walked the path, and then they'd shift over just alittle

speaker: JH
words: ways and they'd change off. And I remembeAound up being the one that

speaker: JH
words: was carrying him in the door. I'd interchanged different times, Iwas

speaker: JH
words: one of them. And the doctor said, "Thanks boys!" And he held up his

speaker: JH
words: hand and he said, "His relatives will be here tomorrow." Well,he was

speaker: JH
words: asingle man. He says, "His relatives will be here tomorrow." Well that

speaker: JH
words: was the first that any of us knew that Charlie was dead! Isuppose we

speaker: JH
words: should have known more, but we didn't. We thought, you know, there

speaker: JH
words: was achance that he was alive, and would be. But that doctor knew

speaker: JH
words: all the time. But Ithink he handled it just right, because the crowd

speaker: JH
words: you know, was getting kind of panicky: do something, do something! Well

speaker: JH
words: everything was being done that could be. He died right there apparently,

speaker: JH
words: because he never stirred.

speaker: JH
words: But then he had lived at Palouse I guess, so the funeral was sched

speaker: JH
words: uled for Palouse a couple of days later. And we had a special train

speaker: JH
words: come up from Palouse and took pret' near the whole town down to Palouse

speaker: JH
words: to the funeral, and of course, had to bring 'em back. I'll never for

speaker: JH
words: get that day either. This engineer, Elmer Helm, he was quite aguy.

speaker: JH
words: Well he seen that big crowd on the depot platform, with asteam engine

speaker: JH
words: you know. And he set up there, the train would run right on by the

speaker: JH
words: depot for oh half amile or more, and then there was aYout there where

speaker: JH
words: they'd Ythe engine. And when he seen that crowd out there- this Helm,

speaker: JH
words: he was kind of ashowman- he must have give it everything it had, be

speaker: JH
words: cause he came by that depot, and that old engine was just a-swayin',

speaker: JH
words: you know. He was putting it as fast as he could. For his own reason

speaker: JH
words: is all! But you know it scared that crowd half to death. But still

speaker: JH
words: for all they wanted to go to the funeral, and they did! They got there

speaker: JH
words: (Chuckling), but there were people got sick on the way down. But I

speaker: JH
words: know they didn't get sick from the ride going down there. They were so

speaker: JH
words: scared with him coming in, they didn't know what he'd be doing on the

speaker: JH
words: way down. But we all got down there and back. But now that's the way

speaker: JH
words: people would do there, you see, just something like that. The whole

speaker: JH
words: town would turn out for it. They were good people, real good people,

speaker: JH
words: generally speaking. 'Course that ended the basketball for the season,

speaker: JH
words: and should have.

speaker: SAM
words: Were sports very big in Bovill?

speaker: JH
words: Oh they always had abaseball team, always. And apretty good one.

speaker: JH
words: And we'd have little leagues made up of, well Potlatch always had a

speaker: JH
words: strong team, Genesee always had agood outfit, they were in the league.

speaker: JH
words: Kendrick always had a good one, they were in. Bovill, and then some

speaker: JH
words: times Elk River would come up with a pretty good team. They had a

speaker: JH
words: sawmill up there in Elk River until about oh, 1930 I think that saw

speaker: JH
words: mill went out. When the sawmill was there Elk River was amuch lar

speaker: JH
words: ger town than Bovill was, and they had apretty good baseball team.

speaker: JH
words: It. went down after that, of course, Oh. yeah,Troy would have a

speaker: JH
words: team some years, and some years they didn't. But we had a league and

speaker: JH
words: real interest in it. The townspeople would follow the team around the

speaker: JH
words: country. We'd go to St. Maries, and sometimes Emida and different places

words: But the league games were interchangeable with these other towns. Oh

speaker: JH
words: we have won the championship there and we've lost too, more times than

speaker: JH
words: we won. But once in awhile we'd come in with a winner. Yeah.

speaker: JH
words: It's a funny thing: Moscow couldn't support a town baseball team.

speaker: JH
words: Too much university there, Iguess. For some reason Moscow never had

speaker: JH
words: a town baseball team. You'd think there'd be enough university stud

speaker: JH
words: ents that'd stay around there during the summer that they could get up

speaker: JH
words: ateam, but no. Sometimes we used to get some guys from the university

speaker: JH
words: to come up and we'd find them a job for the summer and they'd play ball

speaker: JH
words: with us. Red Jacoby was one of them. He was with us, oh I don't know,

speaker: JH
words: three years I think. He later went on, you know, to Wyoming, and he be

speaker: JH
words: came a graduate manager there. In fact he just died about a year ago.

speaker: JH
words: I met a man here in Spokane about a year ago, and he was from Wyoming.

speaker: JH
words: And I said, "Do you know, I only knew of one man," I guess I probably

speaker: JH
words: said, "I know one man in Wyoming, and that's Red Jacoby!"

speaker: JH
words: That's kind of funny, isn't it? There's a whole state, and he did,

speaker: JH
words: he knew him. Oh yeah, we had baseball, basketball, we used to play

speaker: JH
words: volleyball. Yeah. But that isn't so today, you know, those towns now,

speaker: JH
words: they don't have a baseball team. Oh there's probably some of them might

speaker: JH
words: have a basketball team, but it's never organized like it used to be,

speaker: JH
words: you know. We had, as I say, those leagues drawn up, we had rules and

speaker: JH
words: we had officials made up of the county.

speaker: SAM
words: That story about the doctor reminded me of that story that you told me

speaker: SAM
words: about when your wife was having the baby.

speaker: JH
words: Oh.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you tell me that?

speaker: JH
words: Yeah, well yes, that was when our youngest boy Jack was born, on April

speaker: JH
words: 5th, 1931. And we had a doctor in Bovill, I believe that was Dr. Porrall,

speaker: JH
words: I wouldn't just swear to his name, but I think it might have been

speaker: JH
words: Dr. Porrall. He visited my wife on this day which was Saturday, I

speaker: JH
words: think, yes. And, "Oh," he said, "nothing to worry about," he said,

speaker: JH
words: "you've got a week or maybe two weeks yet." So he told her that he

speaker: JH
words: was taking the train to Elk River that day and he'd be up there over

speaker: JH
words: the weekend. So during that Saturday night here the labor pains start

speaker: JH
words: ed, and we knew the baby was coming. So I hiked up to the doctor's of

speaker: JH
words: fice which was the old hospital and asked the doctor's wife to come

speaker: JH
words: down with me and she did. I was awful glad that she did come because

speaker: JH
words: she knew so much more about it than I did. But of course got ahold of

speaker: JH
words: the doctor by phone at Elk River. Well he had no way of coming ft :it

speaker: JH
words: course impassable, nobody could get over it. You couldn't

speaker: JH
words: call it a highway, it was just a road at the time, but you couldn't get

speaker: JH
words: over it. So well, couldn't do anything about that.

speaker: JH
words: But Axel Anderson, some way he found out about that. I hadn't

speaker: JH
words: talked to Axel, he found out about it. And they had this old Dodge bug

speaker: JH
words: that they used to run on the railroad sometimes, nothing but an old Dodge

speaker: JH
words: car with the four railroad wheels on it. But it had been put away for

speaker: JH
words: the winter, hadn't been used again that year, and the battery was out of

speaker: JH
words: it. And he scouted around, and he got a battery and got it in that old

speaker: JH
words: rig, and he got the doctor and brought him down to Bovill. I didn't even

speaker: JH
words: know they were coming. Here they showed up at the house. I was awfully

speaker: JH
words: glad to see them. Well the doctor said everything was all right, glad

speaker: JH
words: to hear that too of course. But I was so excited, you know, I didn't

speaker: JH
words: even ask Axel if he'd like a cup of coffee. I told him about it some

speaker: JH
words: time later. "Oh," he said, "I understand. I had kids too, you know."

speaker: JH
words: I always thanked Axel for that. How he got the word I don't know. He

speaker: JH
words: did find out, but somebody told him. And this was late at night too.

speaker: JH
words: You know, and then when the Depression come on hard, a little worse,

speaker: JH
words: we didn't have a doctor. We didn't have a doctor in Bovill. But here

speaker: JH
words: they had 260 horses across there, and the Potlatch Lumber Company did

speaker: JH
words: have a veterinary. So you'd hear this around town: "The horses are

speaker: JH
words: more important than the peoplel" Yeah.

speaker: SAM
words: Did people go to the vet when they got sick?

speaker: JH
words: Quite often, especially if they got hurt, you know. And he did have a

speaker: JH
words: reputation, he could set a bone probably as good as a medical doctor—

speaker: JH
words: your wrist, or your leg or something. He also knew a little something

speaker: JH
words: have him in jail some place. Oh yes, alot of people would take his

speaker: JH
words: advice. And they'd say for an injury he'd go right ahead and fix them

speaker: JH
words: up until, you know, they could get to adoctor, which would be the next

speaker: JH
words: day. Yeah, that's the way that worked.

speaker: JH
words: Do know what they used to call Axel, what his nickname was?

speaker: JH
words: "The Big Swede." Yep, whenever anybody would say the Big Swede,

speaker: JH
words: Yeah, he was in the snowplow one time on one of their logging roads,

speaker: JH
words: and the goldarn snowplow derailed and went over atrestle, is what it

speaker: JH
words: was, went down there quite aways. Had astove in there and of course

speaker: JH
words: it fell over. It's awonder the thing didn't burn up. Axel, he got hurt

speaker: JH
words: pretty bad. But he came out of it. Ithink he might have broken his

speaker: JH
words: shoulder, but he was awful lucky that it wasn't worse. And another

speaker: JH
words: fella with him, Joe Evans, he got banged up some too, broke his leg or

speaker: JH
words: arm or something, he was laid up for awhile. Oh, I'll betcha Axel's

speaker: JH
words: had a lot of experiences in the woods.

speaker: JH
words: (Chuckles) They had a fella there, his name was Joe Buck. Well

speaker: JH
words: he was, oh Idon't know, ajack of all trades, and hewas sort of a

speaker: JH
words: painter too. But he was working for the Potlatch at the time in what

speaker: JH
words: they called the basin, which is between Bovill and Elk River but off

speaker: JH
words: to the east some. They had, oh about three camps out there I think,

speaker: JH
words: A, B, C, D, maybe they had four camps out there. The logs were trans

speaker: JH
words: ported into Elk River for the mill. Then they had abig coal dump out

speaker: JH
words: there, and the coal would come into Elk River, and then it would have

speaker: JH
words: to be taken out to these camps with the shay engine, oh maybe four or

speaker: JH
words: five cars at a time. And this Joe Buck, he was working out there as

speaker: JH
words: ,an engine watchman. Well that would be at night when the engines were

speaker: JH
words: tied up, he was watching engines. Well it seems like there was no crew

speaker: JH
words: around. This Joe, he was akind of astrong union man too. They didn't

speaker: JH
words: know it though until then. So it was this night and they were getting

speaker: JH
words: short on coal and there, as this story goes, Axel asked him, "Joe,

speaker: JH
words: could you take the engine and run into Elk River and get one carload

speaker: JH
words: of coal?" Joe says, "Sure Axel, sure I can, but will T?" (Laughs).

speaker: JH
words: And he didn't.

speaker: SAM
words: Axel didn't even can him?

speaker: JH
words: Oh no, no. And Ithink it was the sanguy, I'm pretty sure it was Joe

speaker: JH
words: Buck, he was kind of abull cook around the camp one time. Oh, there

speaker: JH
words: was little jobs, you know, maybe they'd have a little pump out here

speaker: JH
words: somewhere to pump water into the camp, or other little duties. "Well,

speaker: JH
words: we'll give you two hours for this or four hours for this," and then

speaker: JH
words: cleaning out the bunkhouses and whatnot. Then they were getting these

speaker: JH
words: extra duties onto 'em, you know, "Well, how about an hour for that?"

speaker: JH
words: Anyway, it all added up to twenty-five hours, and Joe, he wondered how

speaker: JH
words: he was gonna work that, (chuckling) with only twenty-four hours in

speaker: JH
words: the day. Well, I guess they got it straightened out. But you see

speaker: JH
words: that would be kind of along the line of gyppo, give you an hour's pay

speaker: JH
words: today if you take care of this.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you know if the men considered Axel as a big management person, or

speaker: SAM
words: if they they thought of him more as aworker like themselves?

speaker: JH

speaker: SAM
words: Would you tell me what the responsibilities were of your job, being

speaker: SAM
words: depot agent there?

speaker: JH
words: Well, it was just like depot agent any other place. I was the tele

speaker: JH
words: graph operator, of course, and there I did the telegraphing as well.

speaker: JH
words: An agent is responsible for everything that happens in and around his

speaker: JH
words: station. And you had to see that things was going right, and you'd

speaker: JH
words: have to keep in good touch, good relations with the business people.

speaker: JH
words: Those days everything was handled on the railroad, there was no truck

speaker: JH
words: ing. Trucking didn't come along for quite a while afterwards. And

speaker: JH
words: then of course the telegraph, you're manager of the Western Union,

speaker: JH
words: and then the express company, you're the express agent. And with your

speaker: JH
words: help, you know, you had the cashier there, you had to supervise him

speaker: JH
words: and see that he's doing his work right, same with your warehouseman;

speaker: JH
words: you do have to oversee those two, I did anyway, and would be the case.

speaker: JH
words: Well that's about it. Well if you was at a station where there was

speaker: JH
words: a competitor railroad, then as a railroad agent, a lot of your work

speaker: JH
words: would be soliciting business. But that at Bovill I didn't have to do,

speaker: JH
words: because the two railroads were right there in the same building, and

speaker: JH
words: going different difections. So there was no soliciting to do. And

speaker: JH
words: then, of course the mail is handled on those trains, you were res

speaker: JH
words: ponsible for that mail until after it leaves the station. You were

speaker: JH
words: responsible for just everything in and around the station.

speaker: JH
words: Well, after I stayed there for so long you see, and after a num

speaker: JH
words: ber of years, the management of both railroads more or less left me

speaker: JH
words: alone. Very seldom did they ever give me instructions of what had to

speaker: JH
words: be done that wasn't being done or anything like that. They kind of

speaker: JH
words: relied on me to run it up there, and I did the best I could. And I

speaker: JH
words: really enjoyed my work. I think one reason that I did is because I

speaker: JH
words: got along quite well with the management. I didn't try to bicker or

speaker: JH
words: fight with them. That's not pleasant work when you don't have boss

speaker: JH
words: es that you get along with. I didn't have any bosses on the jobj but

speaker: JH
words: here like Spokane, the Milwaukee, this was the headquarters for them,

speaker: JH
words: Potlatch for the WI and M. But I got along with them good, and you did

speaker: JH
words: say you talked with Walt Gamble— he was my boss from the WI and M for

speaker: JH
words: quite a number of years. Got along well with him.

speaker: JH
words: Everything you know, those days came in and out of town on the

speaker: JH
words: railroad. Oh you do things sometimes, probably you shouldn't, but

speaker: JH

speaker: JH

speaker: JH
words: Spokane, and they wanted the body brought into Spokane. Again this

speaker: JH

speaker: JH
words: (mortician, whatever you want to), came out from Spokane, and the

speaker: JH
words: drayman took him across the meadow to this place where the man had

speaker: JH
words: died— and he'd been dead for a couple of days— a day and a half any

speaker: JH
words: way, I guess— while this passenger train went to Elk River and back,

speaker: JH
words: which would take about two and a half hours. And the drayman took

speaker: JH
words: this man over, and he prepared the body over there well enough to ship.

speaker: JH
words: But he thought it was— all he had was a basket, and they're narrow

speaker: JH
words: you know, narrow as a body, well, nothing but a basket is all it is,

speaker: JH
words: wrapped around. And then the drayman brought it back to the depot.

speaker: JH
words: I should have known this, but I didn't, and here we had the body on

speaker: JH
words: the truck and ready to load into the baggage car.  this baggage

speaker: JH
words: man on that train, he was all right, Steve Kinnard was his name, but

speaker: JH
words: he was one that wanted to stay right as strictly to the letter of the

speaker: JH
words: law and rule as he could. And when he seen the carrying basket, that's

speaker: JH
words: what they called it, and no handles on it, he said, "No, no, no hand

speaker: JH
words: les!" "Why," I said, "it can be handled easy enough." He said, "No

speaker: JH
words: handles on it!" Well, I couldn't argue with that, you know, because

speaker: JH
words: there wasn't any handles on it. "Nope!" "Well," I said, "I'll go in

speaker: JH
words: and see." So I went in the depot, and the conductor was still in there

speaker: JH
words: because Bovill was a registered station and the conductors had to come

speaker: JH
words: to register. And I told the conductor, "Can you hold up for a minute

speaker: JH
words: or so?" "Yeah." He didn't know what I was doing,none of his business,

speaker: JH

speaker: JH
words: for me, the superintendent's name." He took the message he took the

speaker: JH

speaker: JH

words: Potlatch. Potlatch see had a good size mill there. One time, I don't

speaker: SAM
words: but you know they had the yards there.

words: But that's all a thing of the past, there's no more sheep grazed

speaker: SAM
words: in the country at all. I don't know why. Somebody told me why, but

speaker: SAM
words: I didn't believe it. You know those sheep tenders or sheepherders, or

speaker: SAM
words: whatever you want to call them— a lot of them were Basques— came

speaker: SAM
words: over here, and they liked sheep and they knew how to handle sheep,and

speaker: SAM
words: just everybody can't. Oh. yeah, an owner told me, "Oh it makes a big

speaker: SAM
words: difference if you've got a man tending herds that knows how and one

speaker: SAM
words: that doesn't." But this fella told me why there's no more sheep any

speaker: SAM
words: more, because those fellas quit coming over here to herd them. He

speaker: SAM

speaker: SAM

speaker: SAM

speaker: SAM
words: I don't pretend to be an expert at all on that, but you keep hogs in

speaker: SAM
words: a sort of small pen and feed 'em heavy, and that's where they put on

speaker: SAM
words: the most weight. But with sheep, of course, they're grazing, you got

speaker: SAM
words: to keep 'em going. A sheep will eat about so much then they lay down.

speaker: SAM
words: Well a herder, you can't let 'em do that. You got to keep 'em mov

speaker: SAM
words: ing. And when they move they eat. Isn't that funny? (Chuckles)

speaker: SAM
words: Just the opposite of the hogs— keep 'em moving and then they keep

speaker: SAM
words: eating. The only time you want 'em still is at night.

speaker: SAM
words: When you mentioned the cedar yard there, it reminded me of Leo, I was

speaker: SAM
words: wondering if you can tell me what Leo was like? I've heard that he

speaker: SAM
words: was a jokester, and certainly was a comic fellow.

speaker: JH
words: I'll tell you, he pulled a good one one a cousin of mine. This cousin,

speaker: JH
words: that was a long time ago, he was here in Spokane, and well, he had a

speaker: JH
words: little job of some kind. He was single and young, he didn't like that

speaker: JH
words: job he had. And Ihappened to be in town. "Well," I said, "if you

speaker: JH
words: want to come back with me to Bovill, I said, "I'm pretty sure you can

speaker: JH
words: go to work in the pole yard." "All right, I'll give it a try." So

speaker: JH
words: he did. And his job was to count the poles on each car. After a car

speaker: JH
words: was loaded,see, then you count the poles to see how many was on there.

speaker: JH
words: They know how many is supposed to be on there, you see, and of course.

speaker: JH
words: the loaders and the checker, whoever it is, tries to get exactly that

speaker: JH
words: number of poles on, but then they always have one to count 'em after

speaker: JH
words: wards. Well the best way to do it is to go to the end where the butts

speaker: JH
words: are, you see, and then count those butts. You can use a little chalk

speaker: JH
words: mark if you want to know that you've counted that. And then you go to

speaker: JH
words: the other end. Well, sometimes you see the poles go that way so there'd

speaker: JH
words: be butts on each end, and the small part would be to the middle. And

speaker: JH
words: this cousin, he was counting, that was part of his work that day. He

speaker: JH
words: was counting and he'd get a different number on the other end -one

speaker: JH
words: more log. And he done it and he done it so-many times that it was

speaker: JH
words: driving him crazy. Like each time he'd count on one end and maybe

speaker: JH
words: hundred Doles and the other end was a hundred one. Well

speaker: JH
words: that doggone Leo, there was a little open space there where there was

speaker: JH
words: nothing, and around the cedar yard there's lots of cutoff pieces, you

speaker: JH
words: know. He took one of those little short pieces that wasn't two feet

speaker: JH
words: long and he shoved it in that space, (chuckles) That made it, yeah.

speaker: JH
words: He'd do things like that. For quite a few years he was in charge of

speaker: JH
words: creosoting. That was part of his work there.

speaker: SAM
words: What is the story you told me about getting his eggs stolen?

speaker: JH
words: Oh yeah. He had a barn, he had cows and chickens. And he knew that

speaker: JH
words: he was losing some eggs. Well he had a neighbor, he was a good enough

speaker: JH
words: fella but he was different, Buck Chambers. And he figured that it was

speaker: JH
words: probably Buck that was stealing his eggs, but he hadn't seen him do it.

speaker: JH
words: So this time, it was early in the morning, I guess it was, I think he

speaker: JH
words: seen Buck go into the barn. And there was nothing to say, you know, or

speaker: JH
words: reprimand him or anything for being in there, that was all right. But

speaker: JH
words: when Leo went in, "How are ya, Buck!" Buck stuttered, you know, and

speaker: JH
words: Leo kind of sized him up, and then he walked over to him, and (slapping

speaker: JH
words: hands on his trousers) on the pockets, and he heard eggs breaking, and

speaker: JH
words: come down on top of his head just playful, you know, down on top of his

speaker: JH
words: head. Here Buck had eggs under the hat, the hat pulled down and he had

speaker: JH
words: eggs up there, and Leo broke the whole works. Oh his pockets, and the

speaker: JH
words: eggs started coming down Buck's face. Well, the jig was up then, of t

speaker: JH
words: course. He didn't do anything about it; he didn't want to do anything

speaker: JH
words: about it. He just wanted to stop Buck from stealing his eggs! (Laughs)

speaker: JH
words: Oh I don't know, stories used to come up around Bovill there, and

speaker: JH
words: somebody would say, "Where did all these new stories come from?" So I

speaker: JH
words: heard somebody say, "Don't you know? Leo Guilfoy makes them up!"

speaker: JH
words: (Chuckles) I don't know whether he did or not. Leo to me didn't

speaker: JH
words: ever worried about anything. Maybe that's why he lived so long.

speaker: JH
words: If he done any worrying he kept it to himself. It couldn't have been

speaker: JH
words: just an easy life, you know, after his wife couldn't be home to take

speaker: JH
words: care of the family. There, he had those three kids, he had to earn a

speaker: JH
words: livelihood as well as supervise the kids until they could do for them

speaker: JH
words: selves. It was a big job, it was a bigger job than most men would do,
